title: Why the brain needs repetition to overwrite traumatic encoding
author: Kalesh
excerpt: I remember sitting in my therapist's office, frustrated. I had been doing the work for years. Journaling, talking, processing. And yet, my body still reacted t...
metaDescription: I remember sitting in my therapist's office, frustrated. I had been doing the work for years. Journaling, talking, processing. And yet, my body still reac...
htmlContent:
I remember sitting in my therapist's office, frustrated. I had been doing the work for years. Journaling, talking, processing. And yet, my body still reacted to certain triggers like I was being chased by a predator. My heart would race. My chest would tighten. My mind would go blank. It felt like I was broken. Like no amount of effort could actually change the wiring.

Here's the thing. I wasn't broken. I was just fighting against the very architecture of my brain. And I had no idea that the key to real change wasn't a single breakthrough moment. It was repetition. Boring, unglamorous, day-in-and-day-out repetition.

So let's talk about why your brain needs to hear the same thing over and over again before it actually believes you're safe. And why that's not a sign of failure. It's just how your nervous system was designed to work.

The brain's survival priority

Your brain has one job that matters more than anything else. Keep you alive. That's it. Everything else, happiness, creativity, connection, those are nice-to-haves. But survival is non-negotiable.

When you experience trauma, your brain encodes that event as a threat template. It's not just a memory. It's a blueprint. Your brain says, "This thing happened. It almost killed me. I need to remember every detail so it never happens again." And it stamps that blueprint deep into your neural architecture.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades studying how trauma reshapes the brain. He found that traumatic memories aren't stored like normal memories. They don't have a timestamp. They don't fade with time. They stay fresh, vivid, and terrifying because your brain treats them as ongoing threats.

Think about it. If you touch a hot stove once, you don't need to touch it a hundred times to learn it's dangerous. One experience is enough. Your brain generalizes that lesson to all stoves, all hot surfaces, anything that looks even vaguely similar. That's efficient for survival. But it's a nightmare for trauma recovery.

Your brain took one traumatic event and built a whole warning system around it. Every time something reminds you of that event, your nervous system activates. You're on high alert. You're scanning for danger. You're ready to fight, flee, or freeze.

And here's the cruel part. That warning system works. It kept you alive. It protected you. But now it's stuck. It's still running the same program even though the threat is long gone. And your brain won't let go of that program easily. It's too important.

Let me tell you something that took me years to understand. The brain doesn't store trauma the way it stores your grocery list. It doesn't file it neatly in the hippocampus with a date stamp and a narrative arc. Trauma gets fragmented. Scattered across neural networks like shrapnel. A sound here. A body sensation there. A feeling with no story attached to it.

Bessel van der Kolk's research showed this clearly - traumatic memories live in the body, in the right hemisphere, in the amygdala. They bypass the language centers entirely. That's why you can't "think" your way out of a flashback. That's why someone can tell you "you're safe now" and your body doesn't believe a word of it.

Notice what's happening in your body right now. Is there tightness somewhere? A heaviness? Maybe a numbness that feels like nothing but is actually something. Your body has been holding this story for you. It's been doing that job faithfully, even when it cost you everything.

Why one insight isn't enough

I used to think that if I just had the right therapy session, the right book, the right realization, everything would click. I would finally understand why I felt so scared all the time, and that understanding would set me free.

It didn't work that way.

Here's what actually happened. I would have a big insight. I would feel hopeful. I would think, "Yes, this is it. I'm finally healing." And then the next day, I would wake up and my body would be right back in the same old patterns. The same anxiety. The same hypervigilance. The same shame.

I thought I was doing something wrong. I thought I wasn't trying hard enough. But the truth is simpler and more frustrating. One insight is just a single data point. Your brain needs thousands of data points before it rewrites the threat template.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, explains this beautifully. He talks about the concept of neuroception. That's your nervous system's ability to detect safety or danger without your conscious input. Your neuroception runs in the background, constantly scanning, constantly evaluating.

And here's the thing. Your neuroception doesn't trust words. It doesn't care about your intellectual understanding. It only trusts experience. Repeated, consistent experience.

So when you tell yourself "I'm safe now," your neuroception doesn't automatically believe you. It needs to see evidence. Over and over again. It needs to feel the safety in your body, not just hear it in your head.

Stay with me here. This is where the repetition comes in.

Every time you practice a new response to a trigger, you're sending a signal to your brain. You're saying, "Hey, remember that thing we thought was dangerous? Turns out it's not. Here's proof." But your brain is skeptical. It's been burned before. It needs to see that proof many, many times before it updates the threat template.

That's why healing feels so slow. You're not just learning something new. You're overwriting something old. And that old encoding is deep. It's layered. It's been reinforced by years of survival responses.

The mechanics of overwriting

Let's get into the actual biology for a second. Your brain is made up of neurons that communicate through synapses. When you learn something, you strengthen the connections between certain neurons. The more you repeat that learning, the stronger those connections become. That's the basis of neuroplasticity, your brain's ability to rewire itself.

Traumatic encoding is just very strong neural connections. Your brain laid down those connections with intense emotion, high arousal, and a survival imperative. So they're thick. They're . They fire easily.

To overwrite them, you need to build even stronger connections in a different direction. That takes time. And repetition. And patience.

Dan Siegel, the clinical professor of psychiatry who studies interpersonal neurobiology, calls this "integration." You're not trying to delete the traumatic memory. That's not possible. You're trying to integrate it into a larger, more flexible story. You're adding new information, new experiences, new responses around that old encoding.

Think of it like a path in a forest. The traumatic encoding is a well-worn trail. It's easy to walk. It's familiar. You know every turn. The new response you're trying to build is a faint deer path. It's barely visible. You have to push through branches and step over roots to follow it.

But every time you walk that deer path, you make it a little clearer. You trample down the grass. You break a few branches. Eventually, that deer path becomes a footpath. Then a dirt road. Then a paved road. And one day, you realize you've been taking the new path without even thinking about it.

Stay with me here. I know this can feel like a lot. The science, the terminology, the implications. But underneath all of it is something simple: your body has been trying to keep you alive. Every symptom you have - the anxiety, the dissociation, the hypervigilance, the numbness - those are survival strategies. They worked. They got you here.

Pat Ogden's sensorimotor psychotherapy approach is built on this understanding. The body isn't the enemy. It's not something to override or push through. It's the primary pathway back to yourself. When we listen to what the body is doing - really listen, without judgment - we start to understand the logic of our own survival.

Right now, as you read this, can you notice your feet on the ground? Can you feel the weight of your body in the chair? That's not a small thing. That's your nervous system practicing presence. Practicing being here, in this moment, instead of back there in the memory. Every time you do that - every single time - you're building new neural pathways. You're proving to your body that now is different from then.

That's overwriting. That's the work.

What repetition actually looks like in practice

So what does this mean for your daily life? It means you have to stop expecting overnight transformation. It means you have to embrace the slow, boring, repetitive work of building new neural pathways.

For me, that looked like practicing the same grounding exercise every single day for months. I would sit in my chair, feel my feet on the floor, and say to myself, "I am here. I am safe. This is now." And for the first few weeks, nothing happened. I felt silly. I felt like I was faking it.

But I kept going. Because I knew the science. I knew my brain needed repetition. So I did it again the next day. And the next. And the next.

Around week four, something shifted. I noticed that when a trigger came up, my body didn't immediately go into full panic mode. There was a pause. A tiny, almost imperceptible pause where I could choose a different response. That pause was the deer path starting to form.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate. Magnesium helps regulate the nervous system, and glycinate form is gentle on the stomach. It's not a cure, but it can support the body while you do the deeper work.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You. Mark Wolynn's work explores how trauma can be passed down through generations and how to break those cycles. It's a helpful framework for understanding why some patterns feel so deeply ingrained.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine. He's the founder of Somatic Experiencing, and his book explains how trauma gets trapped in the body and how to release it. It's a practical guide for working with the physical sensations that come up during healing.

The repetition also showed up in how I talked to myself. I used to beat myself up for still being triggered. I would say, "Why are you still reacting this way? You know better." That was just reinforcing the old encoding. So I started repeating a new message. "This is a survival response. It kept you alive. Now you're learning a new way. Be patient."

I said that to myself dozens of times a day. Hundreds of times. And slowly, that new message started to sink in. My inner critic got quieter. My self-compassion got louder.

Why your resistance is part of the process

You're going to resist this. I did. Everyone does. Because repetition feels boring. It feels like you're not making progress. It feels like you're just going through the motions.

But here's what I've learned. The resistance itself is a sign that you're on the right track. Your brain is fighting to keep the old encoding because it thinks that encoding is protecting you. It doesn't want to let go.

So when you feel like giving up, when you feel like nothing is changing, that's exactly when you need to keep going. That's when the real rewiring happens. Right in the middle of your doubt.

Wild, right? The very thing that makes you want to quit is the thing that proves you're doing the work.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana. She provides exercises and tools for working with your nervous system in a way that feels accessible and grounded. It's a great companion for the repetition work.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that last paragraph. Did you feel a little hope? A little skepticism? A little shame? Just notice it. Don't judge it. That's your nervous system doing exactly what it's supposed to do.

You don't need to fix it all at once. You just need to show up again tomorrow. And the day after that. And the day after that.

Your brain will catch up. It takes time. But it will catch up.
